title: Liquorice root decoction for gastritis
subtitle: A short-course soothing herb for stomach irritation
excerpt: Dried liquorice root simmered for 10 minutes and drunk after meals. A soothing herb preparation traditionally used for gastric irritation and acid reflux. Short course only: 4 weeks maximum.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer), A Modern Herbal, liquorice entry: the gel-forming and inflammation-calming tradition, the simmered preparation method. the herbal reference on Liquiritiae radix, the established use for mucus and congestion and mild gastric complaints. the World Health Organisation Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Vol. 2, Radix Glycyrrhizae.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Dried liquorice root stores 2 years in a sealed container, cool and dark. Prepare each cup fresh.

glossaryTerms.definition: The primary active constituent of liquorice root, responsible for its characteristic sweet taste and anti-inflammatory action, and also for the blood-pressure-raising effect at high or prolonged doses. Deglycyrrhizinated forms exist but are not covered here.

content.text: Liquorice root (Glycyrrhiza glabra) is a soothing and calming herb used in western, Chinese, and Ayurvedic traditions for gastric irritation and mild acid reflux. The root's

content gives it a sweet, anise-like taste. It also carries the blood-pressure concern that makes short-course use the rule. This simmered preparation is made from 3 g of dried root, drunk warm after meals. Course length: 4 weeks maximum.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Hypertension. Glycyrrhizin raises blood pressure by inhibiting the enzyme that breaks down cortisol, causing pseudoaldosteronism at higher or prolonged doses. Do not use this preparation if you have high blood pressure, heart failure, or a history of significant cardiac disease.

Cardiac medication. Liquorice interacts significantly with digoxin (it can lower potassium, potentiating digoxin toxicity), and with antihypertensives (it counteracts their effect). Do not use with any cardiac medication without consulting your prescriber.

Course length. Four weeks at the dose below is the maximum course. Longer use causes potassium depletion, fluid retention, and blood pressure rise.

Pregnancy. Do not use liquorice preparations therapeutically in pregnancy. Epidemiological studies link regular liquorice consumption in pregnancy with adverse outcomes; the safety margin is too narrow for therapeutic use.

Paediatric use. Not for children under twelve without specialist herbalist advice.

items.prepNote: dried cut root, about 1 teaspoon; not confectionery liquorice
cold start; it will reduce to about 250 ml

content.text: Combine root and cold water in a small saucepan. Bring to a gentle simmer and maintain for 10 minutes. The liquid should turn pale gold and smell distinctly sweet and slightly anise-like. Strain through a sieve into a cup. Drink warm after a meal. The preparation is naturally sweet; no sweetener is needed or appropriate (it would increase overall intake).

One cup (250 ml, containing 3 g dried root) after the main meal, once daily. 5-3 g dried root per day. Take for no more than 4 weeks. If symptoms persist after 4 weeks, see your doctor. Take a minimum 4-week break before another course. Do not exceed the stated dose in an attempt to speed results. Paediatric use: not for children under twelve.
Prepare fresh each time. Dried root stores 2 years in a sealed container, cool and dark.
When not to use this remedy

Hypertension, heart failure, cardiac medication: do not use. Pregnancy: do not use. Course limit exceeded (4 weeks): stop and take a break. Symptoms that need medical assessment rather than herbal management: pain that wakes you at night, vomiting blood, black tarry stools, unexplained weight loss, difficulty swallowing, persistent symptoms despite treatment.
